sentences: The 1995–96 UMass Minutemen basketball team represented the University of Massachusetts Amherst during the 1995–96 NCAA Division I men's basketball season.
The Minutemen, led by eighth year head coach John Calipari, played their home games at William D. Mullins Memorial Center and were members of the Atlantic 10 Conference.
They finished the season 35–2, 15–1 in A-10 play to finish in first place.
The Minutemen won the A-10 Conference Tournament by beating Temple in the finals.
They were awarded a #1 seed in the NCAA Tournament.
The Minutemen advanced to the Final Four, losing to eventual National Champion, Kentucky.
The 2006–07 UMass Minutemen basketball team represented the University of Massachusetts Amherst during the 2006–07 NCAA Division I men's basketball season.
The Minutemen, led by second year head coach Travis Ford, played their home games at William D. Mullins Memorial Center and are members of the Atlantic 10 Conference.
They finished the season 24-9, 13-3 in A-10 play to finish for a first place tie with Xavier.
The 2016–17 UMass Minutemen basketball team represented the University of Massachusetts Amherst during the 2016–17 NCAA Division I men's basketball season.
The Minutemen, led by ninth-year head coach Derek Kellogg, played their home games at the William D. Mullins Memorial Center in Amherst, Massachusetts as members of the Atlantic 10 Conference.
They finished the season 15–18, 4–14 A-10 play to finish in a tie for 12th place.
As the No. 12 seed in the A-10 Tournament, they defeated Saint Joseph's in the first round before losing to St. Bonaventure in the second round.
The 2011–12 UMass Minutemen basketball team represented the University of Massachusetts Amherst during the 2011–12 NCAA Division I men's basketball season.

The Minutemen, led by fourth year head coach Derek Kellogg, played their home games at William D. Mullins Memorial Center, with one home game played at Curry Hicks Cage, and are members of the Atlantic 10 Conference.
They finished the season 25–12, 9–7 in A-10 play to finish in a four way tie for fifth place.
They lost in the semifinals of the Atlantic 10 Basketball Tournament to St. Bonaventure.

They were invited to the 2012 National Invitation Tournament where they defeated Mississippi State, Seton Hall, and Drexel en route to the semifinals at Madison Square Garden where they fell to Stanford.
The 2012–13 UMass Minutemen basketball team represented the University of Massachusetts Amherst during the 2012–13 NCAA Division I men's basketball season.
The Minutemen, led by fifth year head coach Derek Kellogg, played their home games at the William D. Mullins Memorial Center and were members of the Atlantic 10 Conference.
They finished the season 21–12, 9–7 in A-10 play to finish in a tie for sixth place.
They advanced to the semifinals of the Atlantic 10 Tournament where they lost to VCU.
They were invited to the 2013 NIT as the highest A-10 team not to go to the NCAA Tournament, where they lost in the first round to Stony Brook.
The 2009–10 UMass Minutemen basketball team represented the University of Massachusetts Amherst during the 2009–10 NCAA Division I men's basketball season.
The Minutemen were led by second year head coach Derek Kellogg and played their home games at William D. Mullins Memorial Center.
They were members of the Atlantic 10 Conference.
They finished the season 12–20, 5–11 in A-10 play to finish in 11th place.
The Minutemen lost to Richmond in the A-10 Tournament.
John Vincent Calipari (born February 10, 1959) is an American college basketball coach.
Since 2009, he has been the head coach at the University of Kentucky.
Calipari was previously the head coach at the University of Memphis (2000–2009) and the University of Massachusetts (1988–1996).
He was also the head coach of the National Basketball Association's New Jersey Nets (1996–1999) and the Dominican Republic national basketball team in 2011 and 2012.
The 2014–15 UMass Minutemen basketball team represented the University of Massachusetts Amherst during the 2014–15 NCAA Division I men's basketball season.
The Minutemen, led by seventh year head coach Derek Kellogg, played their home games at the William D. Mullins Memorial Center and were members of the Atlantic 10 Conference.
They finished the season 17–15, 10–8 in A-10 play to finish in a three way tie for sixth place.
They lost in the scound round of the A-10 Tournament to La Salle.
The 1992–93 UMass Minutemen basketball team represented the University of Massachusetts Amherst during the 1992–93 NCAA Division I men's basketball season.
The Minutemen, led by fifth year head coach John Calipari were members of the Atlantic 10 Conference.
They finished the season 24-7, 11-3 in A-10 play to finish in first place.
It also marked the last season home games would be regularly played at Curry Hicks Cage.
The 2015–16 UMass Minutemen basketball team represented the University of Massachusetts Amherst during the 2015–16 NCAA Division I men's basketball season.
The Minutemen, led by eighth year head coach Derek Kellogg, played their home games at the William D. Mullins Memorial Center and were members of the Atlantic 10 Conference.
They finished the season 14–18, 6–12 in A-10 play to finish in a tie for tenth place.
They defeated Rhode Island in the second round of the A-10 Tournament to advance to the quarterfinals where they lost to VCU.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity and at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that same named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append exactly one brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that passage's linking fact and references the corroborating passage. Perform an explicit identity-alignment check (alias or explicit linking); do not treat mere name co-occurrence or a passage that merely mentions the name as sufficient. Do not append this corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep this addition short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

hop1_docs: 1992–93 UMass Minutemen basketball team | The 1992–93 UMass Minutemen basketball team represented the University of Massachusetts Amherst during the 1992–93 NCAA Division I men's basketball season.  The Minutemen, led by fifth year head coach John Calipari were members of the Atlantic 10 Conference.  They finished the season 24-7, 11-3 in A-10 play to finish in first place.  It also marked the last season home games would be regularly played at Curry Hicks Cage.

1992 UMass Minutemen football team | The 1992 UMass Minutemen football team represented the University of Massachusetts Amherst in the 1992 NCAA Division I-AA football season as a member of the Yankee Conference.  The team was coached by Mike Hodges and played its home games at Warren McGuirk Alumni Stadium in Hadley, Massachusetts.  The 1992 season was Hodges' first as head coach of the Minutemen.  UMass finished the season with a record of 7–3 overall and 5–3 in conference play.

1999–2000 UMass Minutemen basketball team | The 1999–00 UMass Minutemen basketball team represented the University of Massachusetts Amherst during the 1999–00 NCAA Division I men's basketball season.  The Minutemen, led by fourth year head coach Bruiser Flint, played their home games at William D. Mullins Memorial Center and are members of the Atlantic 10 Conference.  They finished the season 17-16, 9-7 in A-10 play to finish in third place.

2008–09 UMass Minutemen basketball team | The 2008–09 UMass Minutemen basketball team represented the University of Massachusetts Amherst during the 2008–09 NCAA Division I men's basketball season.  The Minutemen, led by first year head coach Derek Kellogg, played their home games at William D. Mullins Memorial Center and are members of the Atlantic 10 Conference.  They finished the season 12-18, 7-9 in A-10 play to finish for tenth place.

2017–18 UMass Minutemen basketball team | The 2017–18 UMass Minutemen basketball team will represent the University of Massachusetts Amherst during the 2017–18 NCAA Division I men's basketball season.  The Minutemen, will be led by first-year head coach Matt McCall and will play their home games at the William D. Mullins Memorial Center in Amherst, Massachusetts as members of the Atlantic 10 Conference.

2009–10 UMass Minutemen basketball team | The 2009–10 UMass Minutemen basketball team represented the University of Massachusetts Amherst during the 2009–10 NCAA Division I men's basketball season.  The Minutemen were led by second year head coach Derek Kellogg and played their home games at William D. Mullins Memorial Center.  They were members of the Atlantic 10 Conference.  They finished the season 12–20, 5–11 in A-10 play to finish in 11th place.  The Minutemen lost to Richmond in the A-10 Tournament.

1997–98 UMass Minutemen basketball team | The 1997–98 UMass Minutemen basketball team represented the University of Massachusetts Amherst during the 1997–98 NCAA Division I men's basketball season.  The Minutemen, led by second year head coach Bruiser Flint, played their home games at William D. Mullins Memorial Center and are members of the Atlantic 10 Conference.  They finished the season 21–11, 12–4 in A-10 play to finish in third place.
response.summary_1: Passage [1] states the 1992–93 UMass Minutemen basketball team was "led by fifth year head coach John Calipari." Missing fact: the coach's middle name. Next hop: retrieve John Calipari's biography/Wikipedia entry (search for "John Calipari full name" or the "John Calipari" page) to extract his middle name. Evidence anchor: the quoted phrase "led by fifth year head coach John Calipari" from passage [1].
hop2_docs: John Calipari | John Vincent Calipari (born February 10, 1959) is an American college basketball coach.  Since 2009, he has been the head coach at the University of Kentucky.  Calipari was previously the head coach at the University of Memphis (2000–2009) and the University of Massachusetts (1988–1996).  He was also the head coach of the National Basketball Association's New Jersey Nets (1996–1999) and the Dominican Republic national basketball team in 2011 and 2012.

2013–14 Kentucky Wildcats men's basketball team | The 2013–14 Kentucky Wildcats men's basketball team represented the University of Kentucky in the 2013–14 college basketball season.  The team played its home games in Lexington, Kentucky for the 39th consecutive season at Rupp Arena, with a capacity of 23,500.  The team was led by fifth-year head coach John Calipari.  This team was nicknamed the "Tweakables" in reference to Calipari's comment prior to the 2014 SEC Tournament.  The team was the National Runner-up in the NCAA Tournament, competing in the school's 16th Final Four.  It was Calipari's third Final Four appearance at Kentucky.

Nicola Calipari | Nicola Calipari (June 23, 1953March 4, 2005) was an Italian major general and SISMI military intelligence officer.  Calipari was killed by American soldiers while escorting a recently released Italian hostage, journalist Giuliana Sgrena, to Baghdad International Airport.

Dominican Republic national basketball team | The Dominican Republic national basketball team participates in international competition.  In 2011 and 2012, John Calipari, the head coach of the University of Kentucky men's basketball team, served as the head coach of the team.  The current coach is Orlando Antigua, a former Calipari assistant and a current assistant coach at the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign.  The team placed third in the 2011 FIBA Americas Championship and fourth in the 2012 FIBA World Olympic Qualifying Tournament, one position shy of qualifying for the 2012 Olympics.

Barry Rohrssen | Barry "Slice" Rohrssen (born June 6, 1960) is an American college basketball coach and the former head men's basketball coach at Manhattan College.  A native of Brooklyn, New York, he was named the 22nd head coach in Manhattan basketball history on April 25, 2006.  Rohrssen was fired on March 9, 2011 after going 58-95 over five seasons.  He has also served as an assistant under John Calipari at Kentucky and at St. John's University under Chris Mullin.

Rod Strickland | Rodney Strickland (born July 11, 1966) is an American retired National Basketball Association (NBA) player.  Strickland played college basketball at DePaul University, where he was awarded All-American honors.  He then enjoyed a long career in the NBA, playing from 1988 to 2005.  Strickland is currently an assistant coach for the South Florida Bulls, under Orlando Antigua.  He formerly served in an administrative role for the University of Kentucky basketball team under head coach John Calipari and was the director of basketball operations at the University of Memphis under Calipari.  He is the godfather of current NBA player Kyrie Irving.

Tony Barbee | Anthony Michael "Tony" Barbee (born August 10, 1971) is an American college basketball coach, and an assistant coach for the Kentucky Wildcats men's basketball program.  He previously served as the head coach at Auburn and UTEP.  Barbee led UTEP to a Conference USA championship in 2010 and was named Conference USA Coach of the Year.  Barbee played college basketball for Massachusetts under John Calipari, winning two Atlantic 10 regular season and tournament championships in 1992 and 1993.
response.summary_2: The 1992–93 UMass Minutemen were led by fifth-year head coach John Calipari. His middle name is Vincent (full name John Vincent Calipari), as shown in passage [1].
